title: Linear Maps & Projection
description: Flashcards for linear map conditions, matrix representations, orthogonal projection, Gram-Schmidt, and least squares.

cards.front: **Linearity condition** for a map $h: V \to W$
cards.back:
**$h$ is linear iff both hold:**

1. $h(\vec{u}+\vec{v}) = h(\vec{u})+h(\vec{v})$ (additivity)
2. $h(c\vec{v}) = c\,h(\vec{v})$ (homogeneity)

Equivalently: $h(c\vec{u}+d\vec{v}) = c\,h(\vec{u})+d\,h(\vec{v})$.

*Usage: check linearity by verifying this one combined condition*

cards.front: **Matrix representation rule** â€” how is column $j$ of $\mathrm{Rep}_{B,D}(h)$ computed?
cards.back:
**Column $j$ = image of $j$-th basis vector, expressed in $D$-coordinates**

$$\text{col}_j(M) = \mathrm{Rep}_D\bigl(h(\vec{\beta}_j)\bigr)$$

*Usage: to build the matrix of a linear map, apply $h$ to each basis vector of the domain and convert to codomain coordinates*

cards.front: **Similarity transform** â€” when are two matrices similar?
cards.back:
**$A \sim B$ iff there exists invertible $P$ with $B = P^{-1}AP$**

Similar matrices represent the **same** linear map in different bases.

Similarity preserves: rank, determinant, trace, eigenvalues, characteristic polynomial.

*Usage: diagonalisation $A = PDP^{-1}$ is a change to an eigenvector basis*

$$\mathrm{proj}_{\hat{\vec{u}}}\vec{v} = (\hat{\vec{u}}\cdot\vec{v})\,\hat{\vec{u}} = \hat{\vec{u}}\hat{\vec{u}}^T\vec{v}$$

The scalar coefficient $(\hat{\vec{u}}\cdot\vec{v})$ is the *component* of $\vec{v}$ along $\hat{\vec{u}}$.

*Usage: building Gram-Schmidt step; decomposing a vector into parallel and perpendicular parts*

cards.front: **Subspace projection** onto column space of $Q$ (orthonormal columns)
cards.back:
**Orthogonal projection matrix $P = QQ^T$**

$$\mathrm{proj}_{\mathcal{C}(Q)}\vec{v} = QQ^T\vec{v}$$

Properties: $P^2 = P$ (idempotent), $P^T = P$ (symmetric), $\|\vec{v}-P\vec{v}\|$ is minimised.

*Usage: least-squares solution; attention mechanism as projection*

Given vectors $\vec{a}_1,\ldots,\vec{a}_k$, the orthogonal component at step $j$ is:

$$\vec{u}_j = \vec{a}_j - \sum_{i<j} \frac{\vec{a}_j\cdot\vec{q}_i}{\vec{q}_i\cdot\vec{q}_i}\vec{q}_i, \qquad \vec{q}_j = \frac{\vec{u}_j}{\|\vec{u}_j\|}$$

*Usage: produces orthonormal basis for column space; equivalent to $A=QR$ factorisation*

$$A^TA\hat{\vec{x}} = A^T\vec{b}$$

Solution: $\hat{\vec{x}} = (A^TA)^{-1}A^T\vec{b}$ (when $A$ has full column rank).

The residual $\vec{b} - A\hat{\vec{x}}$ is orthogonal to every column of $A$.

*Usage: linear regression; overdetermined systems*

$$A^+ = V\Sigma^+U^T \quad\text{(from SVD } A = U\Sigma V^T\text{)}$$

$\Sigma^+$ replaces each non-zero diagonal $\sigma_i$ with $1/\sigma_i$, leaves zeros as zero.

*Usage: minimum-norm least-squares solution $\hat{\vec{x}} = A^+\vec{b}$; generalises inverse to non-square and rank-deficient matrices*

cards.front: **Hat matrix** $H = A(A^TA)^{-1}A^T$ â€” what does it do?
cards.back:
**Orthogonal projection onto column space of $A$**

$$\hat{\vec{y}} = H\vec{y} = A\hat{\vec{x}}$$

Properties: $H^2=H$, $H^T=H$, eigenvalues are 0 or 1.

*Usage: $\hat{\vec{y}}$ is the fitted value in regression; residuals $\vec{e} = (I-H)\vec{y}$*

$$Q^TQ = QQ^T = I \implies Q^{-1} = Q^T$$

- Columns are orthonormal: $\vec{q}_i\cdot\vec{q}_j = \delta_{ij}$
- Preserves lengths: $\|Q\vec{v}\|=\|\vec{v}\|$
- Preserves angles: $\langle Q\vec{u}, Q\vec{v}\rangle = \langle\vec{u},\vec{v}\rangle$

*Usage: $Q$ in QR and SVD; rotation/reflection matrices*

$$\mathrm{rank}(h) + \mathrm{nullity}(h) = \dim(\text{domain})$$

For $A\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}$:
$$\mathrm{rank}(A) + \mathrm{nullity}(A) = n$$

*Usage: if $A$ is $m\times n$ with rank $r$, the null space has dimension $n-r$ â€” that many free parameters*

If $A\sim B$ (i.e. $B=P^{-1}AP$) then $A$ and $B$ have the same:

- Rank
- Determinant: $|B|=|A|$
- Trace: $\mathrm{tr}(B)=\mathrm{tr}(A)$
- Eigenvalues (including multiplicities)
- Characteristic polynomial $\det(\lambda I - A)$

*Usage: any of these can be computed in whichever basis is most convenient*

**Eigenvalues of a projection are 0 or 1**

$P^2\vec{v}=P\vec{v}\Rightarrow\lambda^2=\lambda\Rightarrow\lambda\in\{0,1\}$

$\mathcal{R}^n$ decomposes as $\mathcal{R}(P)\oplus\mathcal{N}(P)$.

*Usage: orthogonal projections are symmetric AND idempotent; oblique projections are idempotent but not symmetric*

cards.front: **Isomorphism** â€” when is a linear map $h: V\to W$ an isomorphism?
cards.back:
**Bijective linear map** (one-to-one and onto)

Equivalently: $h$ has a linear inverse $h^{-1}$.

For finite dimensions: $h$ is an isomorphism $\iff$ $\dim V = \dim W$ and $\mathcal{N}(h)=\{\vec{0}\}$.

*Usage: $V\cong\mathbb{R}^{\dim V}$ for any finite-dimensional real space â€” all $n$-dim spaces are "the same"*
